DURAN: Sure, and a carpenter, what are you going to retire--who's going to pay you? The only one that could pay you at carpentry is the union. That's what your retirement is from, the unions. I'm very happy that I retired from the government, because the government pays me my annuity every year; I have a good Blue Shield health plan for my wife and I, you know?
WASHBURN: Yes, it sounds very stable. You started the post office in what year?
DURAN: 1952.
WASHBURN: Was it mixed race in your post office, also?
DURAN: Oh, yes, sure. In fact, one of the councilmen in Richmond, Nathaniel Bates. He and I worked in Richmond post office.
WASHBURN: I don't know Nathaniel Bates.
DURAN: He's a councilman.
WASHBURN: Is he a black man?
DURAN: Yes.
WASHBURN: Yes. So they were hiring all sorts of people?
DURAN: Sure, the blacks, we worked together there. It didn't make any difference.
WASHBURN: Even in the fifties?
DURAN: Sure. That's the time we were there.
WASHBURN: Because people say after the war, because jobs were scarce, people would hire a white person over somebody of color.
DURAN: I don't know about that.
WASHBURN: You don't know about that? What did some of your other friends do that you met here, for work? I know your wife worked in insurance. Do you remember any of your other friends having to leave Richmond because they couldn't find work?
DURAN: No. A lot of friends worked for Chevron. They worked for different factories here. Sure. Who did Danny work for, honey?
EMMA DURAN: Stillford.
DURAN: Stillford Chemical. They worked for all that.
WASHBURN: I have to change this tape.
DURAN: Just forget it, we're going--.
WASHBURN: Well, how about we go for maybe twenty more minutes and then we'll stop?
DURAN: Okay.
WASHBURN: Because we've talked for quite a while, and it gets pretty tiring to talk for this long.
DURAN: Yes, it does.
WASHBURN: But, I only want to talk about a couple other things, and then we'll stop. Actually, I'm going to ask him about the clubs, and you are--
EMMA DURAN: I'm going to go eat an orange or something. I'm not a club person.
WASHBURN: You weren't in any of the clubs? No, no, no, about like the Colorado Club and this kind of thing.
EMMA DURAN: Oh. Well, we only participated in their social functions. We didn't belong to any--
DURAN: No, no, we didn't pay any dues or anything.
EMMA DURAN: Yes, we only went to social functions.
WASHBURN: Well, that's what I mean. That's still something that's, I think, pretty interesting. Thanks for your patience. You've been really, really patient. It's been really a pleasure. So, I want to ask you just about a couple other things before we stop.
The first time we met, you told me about a few clubs that you belonged to, and I wonder if we could talk about those again? You had talked about the Colorado Club, and the Latin American Club. What was the Colorado Club?
DURAN: What was it? Just friends getting together, that's all. Just a regular get-together club. Just people from Colorado get together, see friends, you know. The hombres you knew and everything like that.
WASHBURN: So, who belonged to the club?
DURAN: Just people that you know from your home state, from your hometown. Home state. Most of them were from my hometown.
WASHBURN: From your hometown? Most were from Walsenburg?
DURAN: Yes.
WASHBURN: So did you keep in contact with all these people from Walsenburg over the years?
DURAN: For a while, not very long, though. Fact is I have some that live over here in Oakland and Hayward that I haven't seen for a long time, San Francisco. I think they are all passed away, friends of mine. Most of them have. I have one here that lives in Fairfield, that we were reared together. We speak to each other once in a while, talk on the phone.
WASHBURN: So what would you guys do, for get-togethers?
DURAN: Dances, maybe have dinners or something like that.
WASHBURN: Would this be at people's houses?
DURAN: No. No, it was at dance halls. You didn't go to people's houses.
WASHBURN: You would rent out a dance hall?
DURAN: Yes, that's what they'd do. There were certain people that were running it, that's all. We just used to go down there and join them, that's all.
WASHBURN: And so was it all Hispanic folks from Colorado who were part of it?
DURAN: Yes.
WASHBURN: Oh. That's quite something, that you stayed in contact, as a community.
DURAN: Yes. Well, friends, that we were raised together. We grew up together. You know? Friends, that we knew each other, knew the families and all that. Because my friends were all about my age. We grew up together. That was Colorado, right there.
WASHBURN: What kind of music would you play for the dances?
DURAN: Just regular big time music. Big band music.
WASHBURN: Big band.
DURAN: Forties. Fact is, there was a kid by the name of Hurtado. He used to own a band. They used to call it--he owned a band, and they used to play all over Colorado, and he came here to San Francisco, and was playing here. His uncle was my brother-in-law, Hurtado. The last time I saw him, I saw him at the Miami Club. He had his band playing there.
WASHBURN: Would they play big band music? Or kind of Latin music?
DURAN: No, never heard Latin music in Colorado. They were all big band music. Latin music just wasn't popular, that's all. But "La Bamba," salsa, and all that, we didn't know anything about that.
WASHBURN: So how many folks were in the Colorado Club?
DURAN: Oh, I don't know. I couldn't tell you. Maybe thirty-five or forty. That's all.
WASHBURN: So it wasn't too big, then?
DURAN: Oh, it was just small. And the Latin American Club was only a few. There was only about twenty, and we used to meet at this man's house in El Cerrito.
WASHBURN: The same man's house, every time?
DURAN: Yes, and then we used to rent a hall to have a little get-together. So we did.
WASHBURN: That sounds nice. So they were both just social events?
DURAN: It was social. It was a social deal, yes. Wherever we used to go dancing ourselves someplace, dance hall or something.
WASHBURN: So would you see these same folks from these clubs in different spots?
DURAN: Yes. Like after I joined the Eagles, we used to go dance and eat dinner at the Eagles' Hall.
WASHBURN: What's the Eagles?
DURAN: Fraternal Order of Eagles. That do nothing but--it's a world-wide deal. FOEs they call them, just like the Elks and stuff like that.
WASHBURN: But the Eagles is not just a Hispanic thing?
DURAN: Oh no, no.
WASHBURN: It's everybody?
DURAN: Yes. It's all over.
WASHBURN: Yes, but I mean the one here in Richmond.
DURAN: Yes, we got the El Cerrito Eagles and the Richmond Eagles. San Pablo Eagles. See? It's a fraternal organization, that's what it is. Help--all they do is community work, you know, for people. We go for the Max Baer Fund, Heart Fund, diabetes, we raise money for them. It's a world-wide deal. It's even in Canada.
WASHBURN: Well, how would you compare the Eagles to the Latin American and Colorado Club?
DURAN: Oh, man, you can't compare them.
WASHBURN: Well, I mean compare what you did each week.
DURAN: Oh no, the only thing, the Eagles is bigger and we've done more work for charity and anything else. Latin American Club, that was just to get together to have a little fun or so.
WASHBURN: It was pretty informal?
DURAN: Yes, sure. Fact is, the Eagles right now, on the first of the month, they have breakfast. They have birthday dinners for people, birthday come around a month, you know?
WASHBURN: Do you still go to those?
DURAN: Yes. I used to go quite a lot. Fact is, I served as a chaplain, a chair. My wife was a secretary of the auxiliary. Then I belonged to the VFW, Veterans of Foreign Wars. I was commander of the Veteran Wars 6422 in El Cerrito, a charter member. My wife was auxiliary president, and I was commander in 1973, '74, something like that. I was in the Eagles and the VFW. I belonged to the VFW; I belonged to Disabled American Veterans.
WASHBURN: So you belonged to a lot of different clubs.
DURAN: I belonged to the SIRs, which is Sons in Retirement. All the old fogeys that are retired meet once a month at the Galileo Club for lunch. We only pay for lunch, then that's all. We don't pay any dues. See, all this--you belong to different clubs like that, you get to meet different people, you know?
WASHBURN: Sure.
DURAN: You get mixed with different people that you have seen and everything like that.
WASHBURN: Were your brothers part of the Colorado Club and Latin American Club?
DURAN: No, after they left here they went home, and my oldest brother was county assessor there, and my other brother was a policeman there.
WASHBURN: Why did they go home?
DURAN: I think they wanted to go home over there. They didn't want to stay here. Then our mother was over there too, so they went and stayed with my mother, I guess. I don't know.
WASHBURN: It's just interesting why they went home, and you stayed.
DURAN: I was always away from them anyway. After I went to the CC camps, I was. And they are all gone, except me. All my brothers and sisters are gone, my mother.
WASHBURN: So it seems like they had much more of a life in Colorado than you did.
DURAN: Oh yes, sure, they did. My oldest brother was born in 1900, so when he died he was ninety-two years old. You know my mother died at 104?
WASHBURN: No! That's great genes.
DURAN: That's right.
WASHBURN: So you and all your brothers ended up working for the government in the end, huh? County assessor, police officer, and post office.
DURAN: Yes. My mother worked for the post office. Like I told you the other day, she was the first postmistress in Garcia. She worked for the post office. That's when I was born, in '17. She had that little post office. Small, little building there.
WASHBURN: She worked there in the nineteen-teens?
DURAN: 1917 and '18, yes.
WASHBURN: Do you think her working in the post office affected your decision to apply to the post office?
DURAN: I don't know. I don't know. Just one of those things that happens, I guess. She worked for the post office, and she worked in a department store. See, my mother had a pretty good education. She graduated from some school in Albuquerque. My grandpa sent her down there. She used to say that when she went to Albuquerque, she went on a covered wagon out there [laughs]. Years ago, I figured it out, I said, "Man, that was a long time ago." 1800 and something. During Wyatt Earp days! [laughs]
WASHBURN: So, you don't think her working in the post office affected your decision to work in the post office.
DURAN: I don't think so, no. It just happened, that's all.
WASHBURN: Was she excited when you started working in the post office?
DURAN: No. No. She didn't work very long, see? She didn't get--I don't think she even got any pension from the government. I know she didn't.
WASHBURN: Yes. You know, one of the things we didn't talk about--we talked about what life was like in Colorado, but we didn't talk about holidays such as Cinco de Mayo, or Sixteenth of September.
DURAN: I don't think about those.
WASHBURN: Do you ever remember celebrating that in Richmond?
DURAN: No. In Richmond, they used to celebrate it, yes, but fact is, we didn't even know what it was all about. I didn't. Back home there was no Cinco De Mayo and stuff like that when we were kids.
WASHBURN: Or Sixteenth of September?
DURAN: Yes, Sixteenth of September, no. Through history, we read it, yes. I read through history what it was, you know. In fact, I didn't even know that some of it had to do with Napoleon and all that. I didn't know that. Cinco De Mayo and Sixteenth of September. There's another nineteenth day, what's that? The blacks celebrate the nineteenth day.
WASHBURN: June Nineteenth?
DURAN: June Nineteenth, yes.
WASHBURN: I think it might be emancipation.
DURAN: That's right. There you go. So, we didn't know anything about the other.
WASHBURN: Well, I'm interested, your wife said you guys moved into this home in 1955?
DURAN: Yes.
WASHBURN: What can you tell me about buying the home here?
DURAN: I got it through the GI Bill.
WASHBURN: You got what through the GI Bill?
DURAN: This home. Ninety dollars. I paid ninety dollars down. It cost me $11,500.
WASHBURN: Was that a lot of money for you at that time?
DURAN: Well, yes, it was, but we were working then and we could afford the payments and all that. It's paid for now. It's been paid for for a long time now. So we've been pretty fortunate.
WASHBURN: Sounds like it.
DURAN: We've been. Now I've got my army pension. It's 100 percent disability. They gave it to me after all. I've had about ten operations on this knee after I got hurt.
WASHBURN: Well, why did you guys decide to move into this home here? Why here and not somewhere else?
DURAN: Because this is a fine home that we liked, that we looked around. We were going to El Cerrito, but like the damn fools, we didn't do it. See, we all make mistakes. I could have bought a brand-new home in El Cerrito for eight thousand dollars. Brand-new built. And we came around here and bought this for eleven five. [laughs]
WASHBURN: It was less.
DURAN: I could have bought it for less over there in El Cerrito, yes.
WASHBURN: Oh, you could have bought it for less in El Cerrito?
DURAN: Yes, eight thousand, nine thousand dollars.
WASHBURN: And this for eleven thousand five?
DURAN: Eleven thousand five.
WASHBURN: Well, why do you think you bought here and not in El Cerrito?
DURAN: I don't know. Just a mistake we made, that's all. We liked the neighborhood here, I guess. We were the only Hispanic people living here at one time, except the one on the corner that was, too. The rest of them were all different nationalities.
WASHBURN: Mostly Anglo?
DURAN: Anglos, yes.
WASHBURN: Do you think--
DURAN: Got along fine.
WASHBURN: Everybody got along all right?
DURAN: Sure. We got along fine because we knew, we lived among them so much, you know, could face it.
WASHBURN: Well, I mean, at that time, I think it was illegal, but people talk about certain neighborhoods, when people sold homes, they wouldn't sell the home to certain people because they wanted to exclude them from that neighborhood.
DURAN: Yes, that's right, they did. That's right. That's true.
WASHBURN: But you don't seem to think that that applied to you guys?
DURAN: No, no.
WASHBURN: Why not?
DURAN: Because I don't think I was ever discriminated against afterwards. I never was. I had good friends. Fact is, the friend of mine that I know, that he sends me a Christmas card all the time, is George Miller. I see him, he talks to me all night.
WASHBURN: He's a congressman?
DURAN: Congressman, yes. We knew his dad. We used to go to Gonzales' Restaurant, you know, his dad would be there. The old man Miller. My kids together, she used to buy my kids candy. I know [John] Nejedly. He was a senator from Walnut Creek. Fact is, I've got a picture of him and I and this {Manuel Acosta?} together, took a picture together at the Eagles. See, that thing there. I wasn't, I never have been discriminated against or anything like that in my neighborhood, no. Fact is, when I was working at the post office, that Walt Gatto was a good friend of mine. Louie Figone, he used to go to the post office and bring me a fifth of whiskey, and rye, gave me things like that. Because most of the people, the Hispanics, of Mexican descent and all that, I get along with them, but most of the friends that are in El Cerrito that are over there--
WASHBURN: Were they Anglo?
DURAN: Louie Favero, and Louie Nacole and all that.
Washburn
Well, you said you were the only Hispanic folks who lived in this neighborhood when you first moved in.
DURAN: That's right.
WASHBURN: Why did you move into this neighborhood and not one where there were other Hispanic folks?
DURAN: I don't know, we never got to, we just didn't like it, that's all. We just liked the neighborhood. That's all. I never tried to follow the trend of Mexicans all together, no, I was my own deal. I knew what I wanted. Now, it's gotten full of Mexicans here now, see? The neighborhood while I was here. One person lives across the street right there, Davis. Her and I are the oldest ones here now.
WASHBURN: When did that start to change? When did the white families who lived here start to move away?
DURAN: Oh, my gosh, I don't know. About three years ago, or four. Four, no more than that. Only about seven years ago they started getting homes farther down, away from the city, you know. George Miller's house was just on Nineteenth and Garvin right here someplace, old man Miller. Nello Bianco was over there on Roosevelt Avenue.
WASHBURN: Have you ever wanted to move away yourself?
DURAN: No, what for? Why are we going to pay off a house, sell it, and go get another one, and start paying bills? This one, I don't have any bills, no house bill, just taxes and that's all. It's paid for. Nobody bothers me here. They treat me good, I treat them good.
WASHBURN: Well, why did other folks move away?
DURAN: I don't know. Because they wanted to, I guess. I couldn't tell you that. They didn't move away because there were Mexicans coming here. There weren't any Mexicans coming here. They just moved away because they wanted to. That's the way I understand it, I don't know. Because it's nothing--there's a lot of Mexicans that are scattered all over Richmond now.
WASHBURN: So maybe they wanted a bigger home or something like that.
DURAN: They want to go out to the boondocks, I guess, you know, they want to be out of the city limits or something. And another one, maybe for taxation too, you don't know. Some of them, people across the street, they passed away, and his grandson is the one who is living there now. People over there are the same way that was in the corner. I had a good friend of mine across the street by the name of Code, on Gaynor.
WASHBURN: When you lived here, and you said you ate over at the Gonzales restaurant, would you walk over there?
DURAN: No.
WASHBURN: What would you do?
DURAN: Drive.
WASHBURN: Was that a popular restaurant to go to?
DURAN: It was popular enough, yes. It was on Tenth Street.
WASHBURN: Would you say that that was the only--
DURAN: No, no, there were other Mexican places. There was one on Macdonald Avenue where I made friends. We used to go down there and eat, too. Right there on Cutting Boulevard, there was a friend of ours. We see her once in a while. Her name is Juanita Quintero. She had a Mexican restaurant there, too. They had one over here on Twenty-Seventh, Twenty-Eighth and Macdonald. Name was Rosa, she had a Mexican place there.
WASHBURN: But you said earlier--
DURAN: La Ponce, La Perla, we used to go down and get some stuff.
WASHBURN: And you even said earlier, though, that in Colorado you didn't eat this kind of food.
DURAN: No, we didn't, no.
WASHBURN: So, why were you going to Mexican restaurants then?
DURAN: Because--I don't know. Because we like it, I guess, now. That's the only thing I can think. Just the same as the Chinese food. We never had Chinese food in Colorado. And we go to eat Chinese food now. See?
WASHBURN: Yes, that's interesting.
DURAN: Sure. People eat, I don't know, whatever they go to, I guess. A lot of people, maybe they don't go eat steak in steak houses, nothing like that, but fish houses or anything like that. They just go to Mexican houses and that's it.
WASHBURN: But I think it's a good point that you make, because I think people like to lump people of Mexican descent all in one thing, you know.
DURAN: Yes!
WASHBURN: And they say, "Oh, you go to a Mexican restaurant because you're Mexican and that's your kind of food." But in reality, this isn't what you were used to eating.
DURAN: No, that's right.
EMMA DURAN: We went all over.
DURAN: We went all over. We didn't go to anything like that.
EMMA DURAN: Even at home, at our house, we have beans maybe once a month.You know, we eat the potatoes, the meat, the whatever.
WASHBURN: More American kind of fare, yes? Yes, that's a good point. What would you say to somebody who would think that or would say to you, "Well, you eat Mexican food because you're Mexican and that's your kind of food that you eat." What would you say to that?
EMMA DURAN: We never encountered anybody that said that, so--
WASHBURN: Oh, no, I mean, hypothetically. Just like in an imaginary--
EMMA DURAN: So I wouldn't know what to say, you know. I would just say, "Hey, we're just average."
DURAN: We're just eating what we want to.
EMMA DURAN: We're just average, yes, we eat what we want, when we want, you know?
DURAN: That's right. Because the spice of life is variety, isn't it? That's it right there for us.
WASHBURN: So what can you tell me, I mean, you guys know that I'm trying to write a history of the Latino community in Richmond, and yet, I'm meeting you guys, and you weren't--
EMMA DURAN: We don't fit the mold.
DURAN: We don't know nothing about it, see?
WASHBURN: You weren't as involved in the Mexican community that was here.
DURAN: No.
WASHBURN: But you seem to have been involved for a while in the Hispanic community that came from Colorado.
EMMA DURAN: Well, only at social functions maybe once or twice a year. That's it.
DURAN: That's social. Social functions. That's right.
WASHBURN: How often did the Colorado Club get together?
EMMA DURAN: Only once, I don't know.
DURAN: On Christmas.
WASHBURN: I understand people down by A street and B street, that area, they've written about that as being a Mexican section of town, a Hispanic section of town. Did you ever go to social functions down there?
DURAN: No.
EMMA DURAN: Nope.
WASHBURN: Or go to do anything down there?
EMMA DURAN: No.
WASHBURN: The Rio Theater was down there, though.
EMMA DURAN: Yes, but that's it. That's as far as we went.
DURAN: We just went to the Parallel, that's all.
EMMA DURAN: Yea, we went to the Parallel occasionally.
DURAN: Yes, occasionally. Not all the time. We went over there just to buy food to bring home.
WASHBURN: Where?
DURAN: The Parallel.
WASHBURN: Oh, ready-made food.
DURAN: Yes, take-home.
WASHBURN: But you wouldn't do any shopping down there or anything like that?
DURAN: No. All the shopping was on Macdonald Avenue.
WASHBURN: Oh. But yet, you've stayed in contact with people, Ned, like--
DURAN: People that I made friends with.
WASHBURN: Like Sal Chavez.
DURAN: People that I made friends with, yes, we have stayed in contact with them, yes.
EMMA DURAN: But they are few and far between now.
DURAN: That's right.
WASHBURN: Who else other than Sal Chavez did you meet here? Tony Avila said he remembered you.
DURAN: Oh, he does?
WASHBURN: Yes. How did you meet him?
DURAN: I don't know, just--
EMMA DURAN: At a bar probably! [laughs]
DURAN: At a bar, yes. Could be at a bar. When did you see him?
WASHBURN: I just called him on the phone.
DURAN: Oh, you did?
WASHBURN: Yes. I might go over there in a little bit and talk to him.
DURAN: Oh, where does he live? Thirty-Seventh?
WASHBURN: Yes, Thirty-Seventh.
DURAN: Oh, I know where it's at.
WASHBURN: Yes, Thirty-Seventh, exactly.
DURAN: You know where, I told you, Emma?
EMMA DURAN: I don't know.
DURAN: I told you that at Roosevelt, you know, when we were going to Pino's house?
EMMA DURAN: Oh, yes.
DURAN: They live on Thirty-Seventh on the left.
EMMA DURAN: Yes.
DURAN: I haven't seen Tony for a long time.
WASHBURN: For every person that you have stayed in touch with who is Hispanic, do you have just as many friends who are not?
DURAN: Are not, that's right. That's right, yes. We have many friends who are not.
WASHBURN: Would you say more of one or the other?
DURAN: About the same. I think we have more than Hispanics. Wouldn't you say, hon?
EMMA DURAN: I don't know, honey.
DURAN: Yes, I think we've got less Hispanic friends. Because of the clubs we belong to, I think that's why.
WASHBURN: The Eagles Club?
EMMA DURAN: Yes, but we're not active any more. At one time we were very active, but we're not active any more.
WASHBURN: Sure.
DURAN: You were secretary of the Eagles, weren't you?
EMMA DURAN: I was treasurer.
DURAN: Treasurer of the Eagles, yes.
EMMA DURAN: For years. But now we don't--I mean, we do go to some functions. We go to breakfast on Sundays, on the first Sunday of the month if we're able. But we don't go to any functions in the evening, because it's too dangerous for us to be out when I can't see that well.
WASHBURN: Sure. Well, I think that's it, you guys, we've been talking for quite a while. Thank you so much.
EMMA DURAN: Okay.
DURAN: And thank you.
WASHBURN: You have a wonderful story to share, and it really helps to enrich my idea of what was going on in Richmond at the time. It really does.
DURAN: That's right. I think Tony Avila can help you more on that deal, too.
WASHBURN: Well, sure, I think he can.
DURAN: He was born and raised here, I think.
WASHBURN: I hope you don't think that I have one--I didn't enter this project hoping to write one story about the Hispanic community, and meeting folks like you really helps to--and I don't think it's just me because of my background, I think other Hispanic people may think the same thing. So, meeting with you has really helped to give a more diverse picture of what was going on.
2-00:DURAN: Well, I'm sure glad that we tried the best to help you out on that. Maybe we might not remember a lot, but you know.
WASHBURN: No, you've done very well.
DURAN: At our age, we forget.
WASHBURN: [laughs] You've done very well. I want to remember one more time, Emma, you worked at the gas rationing board in the Kaiser, and then--?
EMMA DURAN: And the shipyard, too.
WASHBURN: And the shipyard too, and then you worked--?
EMMA DURAN: Cal Farm Insurance. Then, at Mechanics Bank.
WASHBURN: How long were you at Cal Farm Insurance for?
EMMA DURAN: Almost ten years.
WASHBURN: Wow.
EMMA DURAN: Then seventeen at the bank.
WASHBURN: Doing secretarial work for them?
EMMA DURAN: Cashiering.
WASHBURN: Cashiering for the insurance, and then at the bank you did window work?
EMMA DURAN: Teller work at first, and then I was supervisor for a while.
WASHBURN: Oh, you worked as a supervisor? How long were you working at Mechanics Bank for?
EMMA DURAN: Seventeen years.
WASHBURN: Wow. So you had a lot of seniority there, I bet.
EMMA DURAN: Well, there were some with more, but seventeen years wasn't bad.
WASHBURN: And that's where you retired from?
EMMA DURAN: Yes.
WASHBURN: That's great.
DURAN: When I retired, I was running Fairmount Station [post office] in El Cerrito.
WASHBURN: Yes, actually I didn't ask you about gaining seniority in the post office. Did you work your way up?
DURAN: Yes, you had to go, well, I was a carrier, then I went as window clerk, working the window, and then I made what you call level six, took care of the window clerks in the main office, and then they put me at the Fairmount Station, and I was running the Fairmount Station there with one clerk, so that's it right there. Then I retired.
WASHBURN: So you became a supervisor?
DURAN: Something like a supervisor.
WASHBURN: And how long did you work for the post office? I think that was from--
DURAN: Well, including my army life and everything like that, four years in the army, thirty-five years.
WASHBURN: Oh no, just for the post office, though, here.
DURAN: But including your age and your number of years, it would be the age of sixty-five or fifty-five.
WASHBURN: So you were for thirty-five years?
DURAN: Thirty-five years, yes. I retired in '79, at the age of sixty-two, see?
WASHBURN: And you said you went into the post office in the fifties?
DURAN: Yes.
WASHBURN: And then you retired in '79, so that's twenty-something years.
DURAN: No, including my army time.
EMMA DURAN: I think he means excluding that.
WASHBURN: Excluding that.
DURAN: Excluding that?
WASHBURN: Yes.
DURAN: Let's see, 1950 to '79, that's twenty-five, twenty-nine years.
WASHBURN: Great.
DURAN: And four years in the army.
EMMA DURAN: You keep wanting to add that four years. I said, "Excluding".
WASHBURN: But why do you add the four years?
DURAN: Because that was included in the credit.
WASHBURN: The credit?
DURAN: The credit they gave me for retirement, see? Because in the army that was known as a federal worker too, you know.
WASHBURN: Sure, right.
DURAN: That's right, the same thing.
WASHBURN: Did you work at that post office for--did you know at some point, your retirement, you were going to get a better package, so you worked a little bit longer?
DURAN: Well, let's face it, it was more for security, security for you and your family. Like you know that you are going to retire with a pension, instead of just--I would have been more leaning to the Social Security pension than anything else. Now, what do you get out of that? Not much. This way I'll get--I'm a triple-dipper. [laughter] Social Security, post office annuity, and my disability from the army, that's all including government work, you know?
WASHBURN: Yes.
DURAN: Army work and all that.
WASHBURN: Did you think about the stability when you entered the post office? Was that one of the reasons?
DURAN: I already had my disability when I did the post office.
WASHBURN: No, in the fifties, did you think about that as being a reason why you wanted to work there?
DURAN: Well, that's the only job I could do, because I couldn't work as a carpenter any more after my leg was hit. I worked because I had to, with a brace, but after they took my brace away, I couldn't walk on the top any more. So the post office was security for me. All right?
EMMA DURAN: Yes.
WASHBURN: Well, great, I'll pack up then.
DURAN: And then I had a great wife.
WASHBURN: [laughs] That's added security, right?
DURAN: Yes.
WASHBURN: [laughs] Definitely added security.
[End of Interview]
